snippet.title: A Cop Can Search You Without a Warrant - Here's the Catch
snippet.description:
A police officer stops you on the sidewalk, has no warrant, and has not seen a crime. Can he still frisk you?

This episode explains Terry v. Ohio, the 1968 Supreme Court case that created the modern rule for brief street stops and weapons-only frisks based on reasonable suspicion.

Visual note: this video uses AI-generated symbolic reconstructions and motion graphics. They are not authentic footage, and no real-person likeness is intended.

Next episode: the phone in your pocket.

Chapters:
00:00 No warrant, no crime seen
00:30 The gap in the Fourth Amendment wall
01:17 Cleveland, 1963
01:35 The store-window pattern
03:10 The concealed weapon charge
03:22 Probable cause vs reasonable suspicion
05:26 The 1968 ruling
05:52 Specific, articulable facts
06:50 Weapons-only frisk
08:11 Douglas dissents
08:33 The modern Terry stop
09:58 The line redrawn every day
10:54 Next: the phone

snippet.title: A Cop Can Search You Without a Warrant - Here's the Catch
snippet.description:
A police officer stops you on the sidewalk. He has no warrant. He has not seen a crime. Can he still frisk you?

This episode explains Terry v. Ohio, the 1968 Supreme Court case that created the modern rule for brief street stops and weapons-only frisks based on reasonable suspicion. It is the case behind the everyday legal question: how much does an officer need before a street encounter becomes a search?

We trace the Cleveland storefront observations, the Fourth Amendment problem, the Supreme Court's 8-1 ruling, and the lasting gap between a hunch, reasonable suspicion, and proof.

This is an educational documentary, not legal advice.

Visual note: this video uses AI-generated symbolic reconstructions and motion graphics. They are not authentic footage, and no real-person likeness is intended.

Next episode: the phone in your pocket.
